basic_info.name: Underfill/Encapsulant Compatibility
basic_info.name_ja: アンダーフィル/封止材適合性
basic_info.description: In semiconductor packaging, underfill and encapsulant materials must adhere strongly to multiple surfaces — silicon die, solder bumps, copper pads, substrate laminate, and molding compound — while resisting moisture ingress and thermal cycling stress. HSP matching between the encapsulant (typically epoxy-based, silicone, or polyurethane) and these diverse surfaces predicts interfacial adhesion strength: low HSP distance corresponds to strong thermodynamic affinity and wetting, promoting crack-free interfaces under thermal and mechanical stress. Poor HSP matching leads to delamination, moisture-driven corrosion, and package failure. HSP analysis enables systematic selection of underfill chemistry and filler surface treatments to maximize adhesion to all critical interfaces in flip-chip, BGA, CSP, and advanced 2.5D/3D packaging architectures.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Underfill and encapsulant performance depends on adhesion at multiple material interfaces within the semiconductor package. The thermodynamic work of adhesion at each interface correlates with the HSP distance between the encapsulant matrix and the surface. For polymeric substrates (PCB laminate, solder resist), direct HSP comparison applies. For inorganic surfaces (silicon, SiO2, Cu, solder alloys), effective surface HSP derived from contact angle measurements or IGC data are used. The encapsulant must have RED < 1 relative to the most critical surfaces to ensure adequate wetting and adhesion. For epoxy underfills, the HSP changes during cure (from liquid monomer/hardener mixture to crosslinked network), and both the uncured state (governing wetting and capillary flow) and cured state (governing long-term adhesion) HSP must be considered. Silane coupling agents are selected based on HSP matching: one end of the silane matches the inorganic surface and the other matches the epoxy matrix, bridging the HSP gap. Moisture ingress prediction also uses HSP: water has extreme dH, and encapsulants with high dH (closer to water HSP) absorb more moisture, degrading adhesion at interfaces through hydrolysis. Minimizing encapsulant dH while maintaining adhesion to surfaces is a key design trade-off.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_enc - dD_surf)^2 + (dP_enc - dP_surf)^2 + (dH_enc - dH_surf)^2); RED = Ra/R0; Moisture uptake ~ f(dH_encapsulant, dH_water); W_adhesion ~ gamma_enc + gamma_surf - gamma_interfacial
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for adequate adhesion to critical surfaces; RED < 0.7 for robust adhesion under thermal cycling; Ra(encapsulant-water) should be maximized (RED >> 1) for moisture resistance [uncertain]

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) of underfill/encapsulant resin (uncured and cured)
HSP values or effective surface HSP of die surface (Si, SiN, SiO2)
HSP values or effective surface HSP of substrate (BT, FR-4, polyimide flex)
HSP values of solder resist and copper pad surfaces
HSP values of silane coupling agents (if used)
data_sources: Encapsulant supplier datasheets (some provide HSP or solubility parameter data)
HSPiP database for epoxy resins, hardeners, and solvents
Contact angle and IGC measurements on semiconductor packaging surfaces
Published literature on epoxy-surface adhesion and HSP correlation
Group contribution estimation for novel encapsulant chemistries
complementary_parameters: CTE (coefficient of thermal expansion) of encapsulant and surrounding materials
Glass transition temperature (Tg) of cured encapsulant
Moisture diffusion coefficient and saturation level
Filler type, size, and loading (silica, alumina, BN)
Viscosity and capillary flow characteristics (for underfill)
Surface roughness of die and substrate

limitations: Real package interfaces involve chemical bonding (silanes, oxide layers) in addition to physical HSP-based adhesion
HSP of cured thermoset encapsulants cannot be measured by dissolution — must be estimated from composition or swelling tests
Multi-material interfaces in a package each have different optimal HSP, requiring compromise formulation
Thermal cycling reliability involves CTE mismatch and fatigue mechanics beyond adhesion/HSP
Filler-matrix interface adds another HSP interaction layer not captured in bulk encapsulant HSP
Surface contamination from flux residues, oxidation, and handling alters effective surface HSP
known_failure_modes: Moisture-induced delamination at die-attach interface where absorbed water (dH = 42.3) disrupts hydrogen bonds regardless of initial HSP matching
Lead-free solder reflow temperatures (260 C+) where encapsulant properties change and moisture generates steam pressure (popcorn failure)
Copper oxidation changing surface HSP over time, degrading initially good encapsulant-Cu adhesion
Low-k dielectric surfaces on advanced dies with different surface chemistry from bulk silicon
validation_method: Button shear adhesion test on individual surfaces
C-SAM (C-mode scanning acoustic microscopy) for delamination detection
Contact angle measurement on package surfaces for wetting assessment

key_references: Wong, C.P.; Luo, S.; Zhang, Z. 'Microelectronics Packaging: Current Trends and Future Challenges', in 'Science and Technology of Advanced Materials', 2005
Zhang, Z.; Wong, C.P. 'Recent Advances in Flip-Chip Underfill: Materials, Process, and Reliability', IEEE Trans. Adv. Packag., 2004, 27, 515-524. DOI: 10.1109/TADVP.2004.831870
Hansen, C.M. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook', 2nd Ed., CRC Press, 2007, Chapter 16 (Adhesion)
Fan, L.; Moon, K.S.; Wong, C.P. 'Adhesion of Underfill and Components in Flip Chip Packages', in 'Materials for Advanced Packaging', Springer, 2009, 437-468

Ardebili, H.; Wong, E.H.; Pecht, M. 'Hygroscopic swelling and sorption characteristics of epoxy molding compounds used in electronic packaging', IEEE Trans. Compon. Packag. Technol., 2003, 26, 206-214. DOI: 10.1109/TCAPT.2002.806172
literature.regulatory_relevance: No direct regulatory requirement for HSP analysis in semiconductor packaging. JEDEC reliability standards (JESD22 series) govern package qualification testing. IPC-SM-785 covers solder joint reliability. RoHS and REACH restrict hazardous substances in packaging materials. Halogen-free requirements drive reformulation of encapsulants where HSP guides alternative hardener and flame retardant selection.

industry_sectors: Semiconductor packaging (flip-chip, BGA, CSP, WLP, 2.5D/3D IC)
Aerospace and defense electronics (harsh environment packaging)
Consumer electronics (smartphone, tablet, wearable SiP modules)
LED packaging (phosphor encapsulation and lens bonding)
commercial_tools: Henkel/Namics/Zymet underfill selection guides (some incorporate HSP concepts)
Materials Studio (molecular dynamics of epoxy-surface interactions)
